retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Enterobius vermicularis, also called pinworm, is one of the most common helminth infections in the world, with most cases occurring in children. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of Enterobius vermicularis infection and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pinworm infestation usually occurs in children. Transmission is via direct contact with contaminated items or even during sexual contact. Most infections are asymptomatic. While the cure rate is high, recurrences are common.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Enterobius vermicularis is an organism that primarily lives in the ileum and cecum. Once E. vermicularis eggs are ingested, they take about 1 to 2 months to develop into adult worms which happens in the small intestine. These do not usually cause any symptoms when confined to the ileocecal area. The female adult worms and ova migrate to the anal area mostly at night time and deposit thousands of eggs in the perianal area. This migration causes pruritus. Eggs hatch near the anal area causing perianal pruritus. This leads to contamination of the fingers and results in ingestion of the eggs (autoinfection) and restarting of the life cycle of the worm. Occasionally, the larvae migrate back into the rectum and to the small intestine and begin the life cycle (retro infection).

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Infection most commonly occurs in children, but any individual is susceptible to E. vermicularis infection. People from tropical climates and school-aged children are the most vulnerable. Infection is caused by the ingestion of the pinworm eggs. Transmission is most commonly through the fecal-oral route. [3] Risk factors for pinworms include poor hygiene, eating after touching contaminated items and living with an individual who is identified as egg positive.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The male to female infection frequency is 2 to 1. However, a female predominance of infection is seen in those between the ages of 5 and 14 years. It most commonly affects children younger than 18 years of age. It is also commonly seen in adults who take care of children and institutionalized children. Center for Disease Control and Prevention data indicates that there are about 40 million people estimated to have been infected in the United States. Transmission can occur via contact with contaminated clothes, bedding, personal care products, and furniture. Fecal-oral is the most common mode of transmission. Rarely, transmission can occur via inhalation mode when eggs are inhaled and then subsequently swallowed.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the etiology of Enterobius vermicularis infection. Describe the epidemiology of Enterobius vermicularis infection. Review the management considerations for patients with Enterobius vermicularis infection. Explain the importance of improving care coordination amongst interprofessional team members to enhance the delivery of care for patients affected by Enterobius vermicularis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognosis following a pinworm infection is excellent. Patients are recommended to follow up with their physicians after completion of the treatment to make sure they do not have any reinfection. Is symptoms recur then testing and treatment as above should be re-initiated.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately a third of patients with Enterobius vermicularis are asymptomatic. The most common symptom associated with pinworm infestation is perianal itching. Perianal erythema may be seen due to the itching and scratching. Sometimes a superficial bacterial infection can occur at the scratching sites resulting in erythema and warmth. Persistent itching can cause disturbances in sleep and may lead to insomnia. Female genitourinary infections have also been reported in the literature. Watery diarrhea has been reported in some patients. Sometimes abdominal pain and other serious complications like appendicitis can occur due to worms blocking the lumen in the appendix or lead to inflammation around the appendix. [4] Sometimes tiny thread-like worms may be visible to the naked eye in the perianal area.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Enterobius vermicularis , also called pinworm, is one of the most common nematode infections in the world. Originally, E. vermicularis was named Oxyuris vermicularis. Humans are the only natural host for this infection. Transmission occurs in people who are living in crowded environments and usually occurs within families. The worms are tiny, thread-like, and whitish. The worm is named after the characteristic pin-like tail present on the posterior part of female worms. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be educated on the need to maintain hygiene and wash their hands regularly to prevent the spread of infection and reinfection.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Preventive strategies recommended are: Washing hands regularly, particularly before eating and after changing diapers. Taking a bath early in the morning to prevent egg contamination should be encouraged in at-risk patients. Trimming of fingernails should be encouraged. Children should be advised to avoid sucking their fingers and touching their perianal area.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Ectopic pinworm infections have been described in many organs including the vagina, inguinal area, genitals, peritoneum, liver, oral cavity, lungs, and pelvis. There are even reported cases of appendicitis caused by impaction of the organ by pinworms. While death is very rare, recurrences are common. Eradicating pinworms from institutions is very difficult and long term surveillance is required. To completely eradicate the pinworm, everyone in the classroom or in the family has to be treated.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Young pinworms tend to be resistant to treatment and hence two doses of medication, two weeks apart are recommended. At the same time, all members of the infected child must be treated. If a large number of children are infected in a class, everyone should be treated twice at 2-week intervals. Follow up is vital to ensure that a cure has been obtained.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Occasionally a superficial bacterial infection can occur at the scratching sites due to intense perianal pruritus. Other complications reported are female genitourinary infections like vulvovaginitis, urinary tract infection in young girls. Appendicitis has also been reported as a consequence of longstanding pinworm infestation.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobius Vermicularis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Enterobius vermicularis infection is usually treated by the primary care nurse practitioner, physician assistant, or primary care physician. Pharmacists have a role in explaining to the patients the side effects of these medications used to treat pinworms as one of the drugs, pyrantel pamoate is available over the counter in the United States. School nurses should also be aware of the manifestations of pinworm infection to prevent their spread in the schools and daycares. [6] Patient education is vital in a coordinated approach by nurses, pharmacists, and clinicians. Parents and caregivers need to be told that all clothes, linen, and towels must be washed with soap and hot water. Hand washing and good hygiene must be practiced at all times. A coordinated team approach to care and followup will provide the best results. [Level V]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1689
retrieved_docs.content: Arsenic compounds, for amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, are classified as agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1682
retrieved_docs.content: Adverse effects of medical drugs are recognized as a distinct category of drug-related side effects, and these adverse effects are specifically classified within the context of anthelmintic agents (Anthelminthica). This classification highlights that the side‑effect profile of anthelmintic medications is considered a subset of the broader spectrum of adverse drug reactions, emphasizing the importance of monitoring and managing these effects in patients receiving treatment for parasitic worm infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1354
retrieved_docs.content: Broxyquinoline is classified as a member of the hydroxyquinoline derivatives used for the treatment of amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1538
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal infection is classified as a bacterial intestinal infection (التهاب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِي جرثومي), indicating that the broader category of intestinal infections includes bacterial etiologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2751
retrieved_docs.content: Quinoline derivatives and related substances that function as antitrematodals are classified as members of the praziquantel group, indicating that these quinoline‑based compounds share the pharmacologic characteristics of praziquantel and are used in the treatment of trematode infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Kambendazol is classified as an antinematodal agent, indicating that it belongs to the group of medications used to treat infections caused by nematodes and is employed in patient care to eradicate parasitic worm infestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: Nitroimidazole derivatives, described as antiprotozoal agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, constitute a pharmacologic class that includes several specific compounds. Azanidazole, nimorazol, propenidazole and secnidazole are all members of the nitroimidazole derivative class, sharing the characteristic nitroimidazole chemical scaffold and being employed in the treatment of amoebiasis and various other protozoal infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1046
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyquinoline derivatives for amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases are classified as members of the broxyquinoline group and are identified as agents used to treat amoebiasis and other protozoal infections. This categorization highlights their role as therapeutic compounds specifically targeting protozoal pathogens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2134
retrieved_docs.content: Antinematodal agents are a class of antiparasitic drugs used to treat infections caused by nematodes, and within this class the filaricida subgroup represents a specialized child category that specifically targets filarial parasites, underscoring the hierarchical relationship in which filaricidal agents are a distinct type of antinematodal medication employed for the management of filarial diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2761
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorchinaldol and broxyquinoline are classified as hydroxyquinoline derivatives that are employed in the treatment of amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, indicating that both agents belong to the therapeutic group of hydroxyquinoline compounds used for managing these parasitic infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2639
retrieved_docs.content: Sulfur containing product ectoparasiticides are classified as a type of ectoparasiticides, a group that also includes scabicides. As sulfur‑based agents, these ectoparasiticides are employed to treat infestations caused by external parasites such as mites, lice, and scabies‑causing organisms, leveraging sulfur’s antiparasitic properties to eliminate or control the offending organisms and alleviate associated skin irritation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1511
retrieved_docs.content: Antihelmintika, also known as anthelmintics, are a class of medications used to treat infections caused by parasites (paraziti). Levamisol is identified as a specific child drug within the antihelmintika group, representing one of the agents that belong to this class. The relationship between antihelmintika and paraziti underscores that these drugs are employed to eliminate or control parasitic organisms, making the connection between antihelmintika, levamisol, and parasites essential for effective management of helminthic diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2890
retrieved_docs.content: parasitic factors may contribute to or modify these diseases. Moreover, parasitology is specifically linked to nervous system congenital anomalies, indicating a possible etiologic role of parasites in congenital neuro‑developmental disorders. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the allowed qualifier unifies diagnostic imaging, surgical planning, diagnostic evaluation, and parasitological investigation across a wide spectrum of endocrine, metabolic, renal, ophthalmic, obstetric, cardiovascular, neurological, and infectious conditions, guiding comprehensive patient assessment and interdisciplinary care.
